title: Dog Head Tilt and Falling Over: Causes and Urgent Triage Steps
meta_desc: If your dog is tilting their head and falling, it could be vestibular disease. Learn why this is an emergency and how to help your pet right now.
content_html:
What is Vestibular Disease?

A sudden head tilt combined with a loss of balance is often referred to as vestibular disease. The vestibular system is responsible for maintaining balance and orientation; when it is disrupted by an inner ear infection, inflammatory disease, or 'Old Dog Vestibular Syndrome,' your dog may feel as though the world is spinning. This often leads to leaning, stumbling, or complete collapse.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Yes, the urgency level for these symptoms is High. While some causes are benign and resolve with time, the symptoms of vestibular disease look identical to more serious neurological issues such as a stroke, brain tumor, or severe toxicity. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately, especially if your dog is also experiencing rapid, flickering eye movements or persistent vomiting.

How Photos and Videos Help Triage

When you call or arrive at the clinic, providing a short video of your dog's gait and a clear photo of their eyes can be life-saving. These symptoms can sometimes fluctuate; a recording ensures the veterinarian sees the exact severity of the head tilt and any eye flickering (nystagmus) that may have occurred at home, leading to a faster and more accurate diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is my dog having a stroke?
faqs.answer: While strokes are less common in dogs than in humans, they can present with head tilts and balance loss. Only a veterinarian can differentiate between a stroke and other vestibular issues through a physical exam and imaging.

faqs.question: How can I keep my dog safe while moving them?
faqs.answer: If your dog is falling, minimize their movement. Use a thick towel or a harness to support their weight and prevent them from hitting their head or limbs on hard surfaces during transport to the vet.

faqs.question: Will my dog recover from this?
faqs.answer: Many dogs with 'Old Dog Vestibular Syndrome' show significant improvement within 48 to 72 hours, but recovery depends entirely on the underlying cause identified by your vet.
